summary: Joel shares his three essential Firefox extensions: IETab for rendering IE-only sites within Firefox, Chris Pederick's Web Developer Extension for testing websites, and Adblock for removing flashing advertisements. He praises IETab for solving compatibility issues with Microsoft sites and the Web Developer Extension as indispensable for web development. His discussion of Adblock leads to a nuanced take on web advertising — he doesn't oppose ads ideologically but objects to flashing ads that exploit evolutionary visual reflexes to cause headaches.
key_points: IETab solves the IE compatibility problem by embedding Internet Explorer's rendering engine directly inside Firefox tabs
The Web Developer Extension is presented as essential for web development, offering quick access to testing tools like screen resizing, disabling JavaScript/CSS, and removing form constraints
Flashing advertisements exploit an evolutionary reflex — human vision is wired to attend to movement in the periphery — making them literally painful to ignore
Joel draws a distinction between acceptable advertising and hostile advertising, arguing the problem isn't commerce but disrespect for the reader's experience
money_quotes: It is impossible to imagine developing web software without this.
I don't have some kind of ideological opposition to commerce or advertising.
Human eyes and brain has evolved together so that your attention is drawn to things that move.
Everything about vision is designed to force you to look at things in the periphery, like lions and snakes and stuff, that might be a threat.
When you're trying to read a page with flashing crap in the corners, you can literally get a headache trying to drag your eyes back to the content that's not flashing.
You're trying to defeat millions of years of evolution and it's painful.
If websites and advertisers have so little respect for me that they are willing to subject me to headaches just to sell a few more dancing hamsters, well, to hell with 'em.
key_insight: Flashing web advertisements are not merely annoying but fundamentally hostile to users, exploiting millions of years of visual evolution to hijack attention at the cost of physical discomfort.
